Transcrição Completa: 2 Lessons From Scaling Agency to 20MM - Chris Walker     [00:00]  "Thanks, brother. Take it away." "Yeah. So, the first thing I want to say is I wish that when I was doing a 100K a month, I was in a group like this because I don't think a lot of the two or three years that I went through that fuck sucked would have happened had I had an education and the the uh wisdom from Jeremy and all the people in the group."   [00:28]  "So, um when I get started, I just want to get a sense of the group because there's a lot of new people here that I don't know. So, uh, raise your hand if you're doing more than 50k a month in profit, not revenue profit."   [00:45]  "And then keep it keep it up if it's more than 100k a month. And then keep it up if it's more than 250k a month. And then keep it up if it's more than 500k a month. fuck yeah. All right. Cool. So, a majority of the group, uh, you know, less than 100k a month in in cash flow."   [01:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Wins, Losses, Learnings - Scaling Agency"   NÚMEROS:  $1.9M/mês no pico   VISUAL:  Título da apresentação sobre journey de scaling   [01:12]  "Um, cool. So, I'm here to talk about the wins, the losses, the learnings of scaling my agency. At the peak, we were doing $1.9 million a month. Um, and during that time, we were doing $1.9 million a month. We were doing 100k in profit."   [01:32]  "So, a fuckload of revenue and almost no profit because I hired a lot of people. I had a lot of fixed costs. Um, and I got a lot of smarter over the time. So, I'm trying to help essentially everyone avoid a lot of the mistakes that I made because I wish I knew a lot of these things when I started."   [01:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Current Picture - 2024/2025"   NÚMEROS:  $12.4M annual revenue, $2.6M net income (20-25% margin), $640K average client, 5 clients >$1M/year, 45 employees, $150K/month personal cashflow   VISUAL:  Snapshot atual do negócio com múltiplas métricas   [02:00]  "So, first off, the picture right now, I've never been in a better spot in my entire life. How I feel, how my businesses are doing, things like that. So, I just want to give you a set of the current picture."   [02:15]  "Uh, my agency this year is on track to do uh 12.4 million, which is effectively a million dollars a month. Um, for on a net income basis, 2.6 mil, 20% margin. Maybe we get it to 24 25% margin."   [02:35]  "Uh, our average client pays us 640 grand a year on retainers, not on revenue shares. So, I can talk through that or answer questions on that. Um, and we have five clients right now that pay us more than a million dollars a year."   [02:52]  "Um, and we have 45 full-time employees totally remote in the US and Canada. Um, when my agency peaked, we had 125 people. And I got to say, more people, more problems."   [03:10]  "And so one of the big big learnings is get as efficient as you can. Get as profitable as you can. Only hire people when you absolutely need them."   [03:22]  "Um and then on a personal distribution side, I don't own 100% of the company anymore. I can talk through that. Uh but I own a majority and I'm expecting uh about 150k a month in cash flow and I don't do anything in the agency except for go to a board meeting once a quarter."   [03:45]  "So that's the current picture."   [03:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2019 - The Beginning"   NÚMEROS:  $3K in bank, $100/hour starting rate, $5K/month retainers, $20K/month first milestone, $1M/year goal hit in 8 months   VISUAL:  Timeline do início da jornada   [03:52]  "Uh now let's talk through the whole journey. So 2019, I started my agency. I had $3,000 in my bank account. Uh I had to sell my car because I couldn't afford the insurance and the parking. I started taking the subway."   [04:12]  "Um I started implementing daily habits up until that point. Before that, I was fuck around on weekends, hanging out with the wrong people, doing the wrong things. Um and started getting my getting my fuck together."   [04:30]  "So consistent sleep, morning workouts, no fuck around on weekends. I work out worked seven days a week and I worked out seven days a week. And some of those things were foundational long before I made money and had success."   [04:48]  "So people that are looking for to get off the ground, working on yourself is the number one thing to get going and then the business stuff will go with it."   [05:02]  "Uh I didn't know how to price it at the beginning. I started at 100 bucks an hour in 2019. We eventually got to 5k a month retainers. Um we I hit 20K a month and I thought that was like the best thing ever. It was just me in the business at that point."   [05:25]  "And all of a sudden you're making a quart million dollar a year, way more than I ever made before. I started thinking bigger and at that point my think big was I can't wait to make a million million bucks a year. I did I hit that uh eight months later."   [05:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Finding ICP - Ideal Customer Profile"   NÚMEROS:  $2K/month contracts iniciais   VISUAL:  Evolução de cliente genérico para software companies específico   [05:50]  "Um I started to try to figure out my ideal customer profile. So at the beginning I was selling 2K a month contract to an ecom business and then uh professional services and I would coach an agency and I would sell a software companies. I was all over the place."   [06:12]  "Um and eventually settled on uh like high growth venture funded and private equity based software companies as my my target profile. very good for some reasons. They get tons of funding from investors and that money then can get passed on right to agencies and advertisers like myself."   [06:35]  "Um but when the faucet gets turned off, when the investors start stop giving money to those companies, the faucet then also stops to me. And I can talk through how that happened."   [06:48]  "I got my first three clients through uh just my personal network, people I'd worked in the past, people that trusted me. And then I started to post on LinkedIn in uh April of 2019. Started to get some customers that way. And I a lot of people want to know about that. So I'll make sure I cover some of those details."   [07:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "First Hires"   NÚMEROS:  $50K/month quando começou a contratar   VISUAL:  Estrutura inicial de contratações   [07:20]  "Um and then I later in that uh year I was at like 50k a month and started to make my first couple of hires. So my first v hire was a video editor to make my LinkedIn content work better. Um and then I also hired someone to do advertising and take on some of my customers so I could focus on sales and marketing."   [07:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2020-2021 - The Boom"   NÚMEROS:  $600K (2019), $2.2M (2020), $6.8M (2021), $21M peak (2022), 10% equity sold at $2.6M valuation for $250K   VISUAL:  Gráfico de crescimento explosivo   [07:50]  "Uh the next period of time was 2020 and 2021. So after the little COVID lockdown, uh June 2020, uh software boomed, my target customers getting all this money, these crazy valuations, they're giving it to me. My company's rapidly scaling."   [08:15]  "So we go from 600K in 2019, 2.2 in 2020, 6 something, 6.8 in 2021, and then peaked at $21 million in annual revenue in 2022."   [08:32]  "Um I added a business partner in early 2020. Um, I sold I sold 10% equity. I didn't give away the equity. That's another big thing for people. Once you create value in your business, don't fuck give it away to someone. Um, make make them buy it."   [08:52]  "So, she bought in at a at a 2.6 million valuation. I put 250K in my in my pocket and then I had her invested in the company at 10%."   [09:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Service Expansion"   NÚMEROS:  $40K-50K/month retainers   VISUAL:  Adição de serviços: Media → Creative/Content → Analytics   [09:12]  "Um, we started to add different services. So, at first it was just media and then our customers sucked at creative and they couldn't do fuck content. So, we started building creative and content out. um and also analytics and that allowed us to increase retainers."   [09:35]  "So all of a sudden we're charging 50k a month, 40k a month in retainers um which uh obviously made the scalability way different than charging 10k a month. So finding ways that you can uh increase uh retainers or however you make money."   [09:55]  "Um we started moving up market the seed and the series A companies are small companies. I fuck hated working with them. They had no money. Their products sucked. They didn't know what to do. They would churn after six months."   [10:15]  "We started moving to more mature companies. Now we work with mainly 50 million to 500 million a billion dollar revenue software companies. Some are publicly traded. Most are privately owned by private equity."   [10:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Podcast & LinkedIn Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  Fastest growing B2B digital agency ever (segundo LinkedIn)   VISUAL:  Timeline de content strategy   [10:38]  "Um I want to talk through on the the podcast in LinkedIn. I did my first uh I started posting on LinkedIn in 2019. I did my first podcast episode the first week that co lockdown happened and that was what drove explosive growth and customer acquisition in my in my company."   [11:02]  "Um and according to LinkedIn for those period of time we were the fastest growing B2B digital agency ever. Um which was fire."   [11:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "LinkedIn Following Growth"   NÚMEROS:  Massive following (número específico não mencionado)   VISUAL:  Screenshots de perfil LinkedIn com engagement   [11:20]  "Uh little bit on the podcast LinkedIn strategy. I took some screenshots here. I've amassed quite a following on LinkedIn. Um it's not exactly as cool as Justin Waller and some of the folks in here that are able to amass a massive following on Instagram or Tik Tok, but on LinkedIn a lot of people have fuck money."   [11:42]  "Um and so I thought like that worked really well for me in terms of the content."   [11:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "First YouTube Video - March 27, 2020"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Screenshot de primeiro vídeo com AirPods, produção básica   [11:52]  "And then I did my first YouTube video. Uh that was like March 27th, 2020 in the COVID lockdown. As you can see, I had AirPods in. The production value sucked. I've gotten a lot better over time."   [12:10]  "But um when you're trying to get started on this, don't let the production and the camera quality and what mic should I choose slow you down. It's really the quality of the information. And it's incredible how fast you get better at doing it, especially when you do it every day."   [12:32]  "Um, and I was really committed to the strategy. At the peak, I was doing six podcast episodes a week. Still today, I do uh three podcast episodes published per week."   [12:48]  "Um, short form content, seven days a week on LinkedIn. U started to expand into Tik Tok and YouTube and other channels, but LinkedIn podcast, that combo is continuously like where I make most my money and get most of my clients."   [13:10]  "Um, and uh, a little note here into Jeremy's stuff. Find channels where rich people hang out, where your customers and rich people hang out. This is where the story."   [13:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2022 - Getting Punched In The Mouth"   NÚMEROS:  -$1.6M net income loss, $21M revenue, 75 layoffs, ~$1M in severances, -$200K/month burn rate   VISUAL:  Gráfico mostrando queda dramática de lucro   [13:30]  "So, everything's sunshine and rainbows for the first three years. And then I get fuck punched in the mouth. Um, in 2022, they call it SAS winter. Now, looking back on it, um, the Salesforce stock bottomed out in December of 2022. There were other industries that got hit as well."   [13:55]  "Um but basically all of our customers were now instructed stop spending money and what's the easiest place to stop spending money on advertising and big a big expensive agency. So that was the first thing to get cut before they would cut headcount and do layoffs and things like that. Marketing is the first thing to get cut."   [14:20]  "Um and so our clients are breaking annual agreements. So they're saying we're supposed to pay you for the next 12 months but now we're we have to we can't pay you next month so fuck off."   [14:38]  "um other customers were churns. We had this big massive amount of churn. This isn't revenue. This is net income on that side. So in 2022, you can see business has been profitable and scaling. And then all of a sudden I I lose $1.6 million uh in my business in 2022"   [15:02]  "Um at that point we were doing 1.9 million in revenue. So if you can imagine my company's doing 20 did $21 million in revenue and I lost$ 1.6 6 million that year and dug myself a massive hole."   [15:22]  "Um, a lot of that was in severances. So, I paid almost a million dollars in severances to the 75 people that I had to lay off over two waves of uh of layoffs."   [15:38]  "Um, and about Yeah, if you can imagine looking at your financial model and your bank account knowing you're losing $200,000 a month, that was pretty much what was happening to me. And then I would lay people off and think it was going to get better and then more clients would churn and you're in this downward spiral of just getting punched in the face."   [16:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Debt Lifeline"   NÚMEROS:  $4M debt raised April 2022, 15% interest rate, interest-only first year, $5M in bank → $3M after year   VISUAL:  Estrutura do debt financing que salvou a empresa   [16:10]  "Um, it was a blessing in disguise because I definitely would have uh gone bankrupt and gone out of business had I not raised $4 million in debt in April of 2022, right before things started getting bad. It was like somebody was watching out for me or something."   [16:32]  "Um, so I raised four million I raised $4 million in debt mezd uh not no one else a traditional bank would have given us money because we didn't have enough history and our revenue is weird and things like that."   [16:50]  "So, uh, 15% interest rate, $4 million loan, interest only for the first year, and then accelerated payments in a balloon at the end."   [17:05]  "Um, and so if I didn't have that extra cash in the bank, then I would have given no severances to employees and I probably would have run out of cash somewhere in the summer of 2022."   [17:22]  "Um, but I did have that. So anyway, uh basically imagine that you get that debt. So all of a sudden you have $5 million in your bank account and at the end of the year you still have to pay all that debt and it's collecting interest and now all of a sudden you have $3 million in your bank account."   [17:42]  "So it switched where uh basically my asset to liabilities ratio was negative. The company was theoretically insolvent."   [17:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2023 - Contemplating Giving Up"   NÚMEROS:  December 20, 2022 (birthday), $15M revenue mas business insolvent, $100K+ gasto em trading   VISUAL:  Momento mais baixo da jornada   [17:58]  "2023 was uh started in uh on my birthday actually which was uh December 20th of 2022 was the first time I contemplated giving up. Um it felt a little bit hopeless at a time."   [18:18]  "Imagine the business is insolvent technically. Uh you're just getting punched in the face every left and right. You don't know if this is going to end, when it's going to end, what's going to happen. In the meantime, my company still does like $15 million in revenue, right? So, I have a massive asset, but emotionally and like financially, I'm just getting getting totally taken down."   [18:45]  "Um, at that point, I we were scaling so fast that I basically taken no money out of the business. Um, and so a big lesson here, like every time, get profit, take it out, stash it in other investments, get profit, take it out, stash it in other investments the whole way."   [19:08]  "I was all investing, taking my money and investing it back into the business and not taking profit out. And I if I could go back, I would never never do that again."   [19:22]  "Um, so if you can imagine, I'm my business is doing more than a million dollars a month and I'm making basically no money and I've taken no money out of the business for the past three years and the balance sheet is underwater."   [19:40]  "Um, and I'm trying to dig out of this hole with my business partner. Um, in the meantime, I'm having conflict with my business partner. Two people that had originally been like the best partners ever and all of a sudden like our business is fuck tanking and there's a bunch of bunch of conflict amongst us."   [20:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Jeremy Call - August 2023"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Momento de virada através de mentoria   [20:10]  "Um, Omar invited me to join the inner circle around April of 2023 and I came to these masterminds a bunch of times and I uh uh in the summer of 23 after I uh fuck around with that trading stuff for a while and spent hundred more than $100,000 and made no money."   [20:35]  "Um I uh was contemplating giving up and giving up would mean that I would go bankrupt because I owed the bank a bunch of money and we didn't have all the assets to pay it back."   [20:50]  "Um, I had a call with Jeremy in August 23. Changed my life. Changed the trajectory of my everything. Um, I'm trying to remember. Maybe Jeremy can come and tell me tell me exactly what he said to me. But it was something to the lines of, uh, you remember what you said to me that time? I do."   [21:15]  "Remember when we had that call in August when I was talking to you about uh, Sean and what was going on?" "Oh, yeah. Drop out. It wasn't really about the Yeah, the dropout was one thing, but you were like, 'Bro, you got a fuck $15 million business. Why are you going to burn that down right now? You're doing a million dollars a month.'"   [21:40]  "And then you said, 'You got to pick a fuck lane, dude. You either have to cash flow it or you have to build it to sell it. You're in the middle right now. And you're doing fuck neither of them, right?' Um, and that simple advice was that and what I paid for this group paid off a trillion times." "good."   [22:05]  "It was probably the best in the whole group. Um, and from that point forward, I totally changed the strategy of what I'm doing in the agency and things like that."   [22:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Recovery - End of 2023"   NÚMEROS:  Quase zerou o buraco financeiro   VISUAL:  Gráfico de balance sheet se recuperando   [22:25]  "So, you can see we're not losing. Uh, that's the balance sheet. So, at the end of 23, we had almost fully dug out of the hole that we had dug ourselves."   [22:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2024 - New Structure"   NÚMEROS:  +16.5% equity grant (total 26.5% para partner), 8-figure exit projetado, 3-year plan   VISUAL:  Nova estrutura de ownership e operação   [22:45]  "Um, and then starting in 2024, I decided that uh I was going to put my business partner in charge of the agency. I didn't even know that was an option at the beginning. I thought, oh, I if I own a majority share of this thing, I have to be the CEO."   [23:08]  "Um, and uh, the reality was that she would have been better at running it and I wanted to do other things that I was way more interested in. And so I put her in charge in January 24."   [23:25]  "I granted her an additional 16.5% equity so that she's been incentivized. We had a a three-year plan to grow the business and then sell it in three years. Um, that gets her up to 26.5%. So it basically did the math. If we we do what we say, she'll have an 8 figure exit. She's properly incentivized."   [23:50]  "Um, and I essentially used that equity that I had to be able to buy back all my time."   [24:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "PTO - SaaS AI Company Launch"   NÚMEROS:  $1.2M first year revenue, $2.5-3M projected year 2, $5-10M revenue goal, $50M+ exit target   VISUAL:  Novo venture em SaaS   [24:10]  "Um, and with that in January 24, I started a SAS AI company called PTO. That company's now up to we did 1.2 million in revenue our first year. Hoping to do 2.5 to three uh this year."   [24:30]  "And the quality and the valuation of a SAS business is way different than a services business. So my goal on that SAS business is to get it somewhere between 5 and 10 million in revenue and sell it for 50 million or more."   [24:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Debt Refinance - Game Changer"   NÚMEROS:  $180K/month → $24K/month payments, $150K/month net income unlocked, $461K Q4 distribution, $500K/quarter projected, $400K salary covenant   VISUAL:  Impacto dramático do refinanciamento   [24:55]  "Um there was a big thing here where like our like I mentioned before our debt payments it was interest only for the first year and then the in the payments increase every single 12 months and at the end you have a balloon which is a big amount you have to pay at the end which is about 2 million 1.6 million in total"   [25:20]  "and so the the debt payments keep increasing and when you pay make debt payments then all of a sudden your net income goes down. So we had no money to to pay shareholders."   [25:38]  "Um, and there was covenants that Jeremy mentioned. So, I could only could pay myself a certain salary. It was it was actually 400k. Me and my business partner couldn't be paid more than 400k in salary. We couldn't take any distributions. Even if our company was making profit, we couldn't take distributions. There was like 15 or 20 other things that we couldn't couldn't do or we weren't restricted to do."   [26:10]  "Um, and so we refinanced our debt to a traditional bank in uh in the summer of 2024. our debt payments went from 180,000 a month to $24,000 a month and all that additional cash flow now just gets profit that can go to shareholders. So it's totally changed everything."   [26:35]  "Um so $150 150k in net income unlocked to shareholders every single month. Q4 was the first full quarter that that happened. So, the debt payments had been changed and I got a $461,000 wire on Wednesday for the distributions of the profit for Q4 and I'm expecting somewhere around 500K per quarter going forward um without being involved in the business almost at all. Yeah."   [27:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "2025 And Forward"   NÚMEROS:  $150K/month agency, $50K/month SaaS profit, $85K/month SaaS revenue, $25K/month passive investments   VISUAL:  Portfolio diversificado de income streams   [27:15]  "And so, what does 2025 and forward look like? Uh I now have a massive cash flowing asset even though the journey was fuck terrible and really hard."   [27:32]  "Um, I feel like, uh, as an entrepreneur at some point you're going to get punched in the mouth and it's going to be hard and you're going to have the option of and I I don't know, it's hard to say. We were talking about this before like you're not really an entrepreneur until you get really fuck punched in the mouth, I think."   [27:55]  "And so it was sunshine and rainbows for me for the first year and then or for the first three years and all of a sudden boom boom boom two or three years of uh really hard stuff. Um, so just be ready for that."   [28:15]  "In the meantime, as well, I people tried to sue me twice. Two of two software vendors, non-C customers, partners tried to sue me. Um, and a lot of other fuck happened."   [28:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Revenue Chart - The Journey"   NÚMEROS:  Crescimento consistente exceto 2022   VISUAL:  Gráfico mostrando crescimento com dip em 2022   [28:38]  "Um, but anyway, you can see the chart. We had one really down year, but on a like have consistently seen that growth. You take out 2022, actually, the business did really well."   [28:55]  "Um, the agency pays me about 150k a month, but it comes out quarterly, so you can do the math on that. Um, we're starting to focus only on this upper market client. So, my business partner is only focused on getting million-dollar a year clients."   [29:18]  "Um, so that's like the 2005$500 million software company. My SAS AI company's getting off the ground. We do 50K a month in profit, about 80 85k a month in revenue. Um, and that one I'm I'm building to to exit. So, I'm building to sell that one. The agency I'm I'm building to cash flow."   [29:45]  "Um, and then I have investments in other businesses and as well as public markets, but I make investments in uh small business professional services type of like agencies or specialized agencies. Uh, and those pay me 25K a month passive and then I have all my public market investments."   [30:10]  "Um, and another key learning was just to if you if you only have your agency, then basically all of your net worth is tied up in one illquid asset. And so being smart about when to derisk, when to get money out of the agency and put it in other places"   [30:32]  "selling 10% to my business partner got me 250k that I started to stash away. And figuring out how to have a balanced d-risk portfolio instead of having all your money tied up in one illquid asset."   [30:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Key Learnings"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplos insights   VISUAL:  Lista de lições principais aprendidas   [30:55]  "So key learnings and then I uh I tried to go quick through this so we could do questions. Honestly, I think questions where we get the real fuck going. So, um, I encourage people if they're thinking about something or have a question to ask it after this slide."   [31:15]  "Um, key learnings for me, there's a difference between being the owner of a company and being an operator. Sometimes you can do both, but you don't have to. I always thought if I own the company that I had to be the CEO."   [31:35]  "Um, and it's a whole different game when you own it and somebody else is the CEO. Way fuck better. Um, and I had a lot of pride tied up in being the CEO of the agency. And now I have it's totally different for me. I didn't know this was possible."   [31:55]  "Um, your first business doesn't have to be your only business. I think a lot of people need a stepping stone in their business to be able to get them industry reputation, business experience, financial security, and then you can take that and use it to try to have a big winner after that."   [32:18]  "Um, dominating organic content works super well for me and is a is a massive asset for the rest of my life. Tons of people know who I am. They trust me. We saw the trust diagram up there."   [32:38]  "Um, it's also like just my time invested. I invest three hours of time every single week to record the content, 30 minutes a day to post and produce the content. Um, and becomes a probably the highest ROI thing that I do in my week every single week. It drives businesses to all my investments to my agency and out of my software company."   [33:08]  "Um, there's a huge difference between cash flow and enterprise value. In agencies, it's kind of the same. your business gets valued off of IBIDA. Um, but in other businesses like our customers, they lose money every single month and the through the growth of their revenue software companies their value goes up."   [33:35]  "And I kind of got caught in that trap thinking if I just grow revenue and even if I lose money, my business will be valuable. That's not the case in agencies for the most part. Um, and so pick a lane and make sure that the lane that you pick matches what what you are or where you belong."   [33:58]  "For most people here, it's going to be a cash flow business and you're going to get valued off of the ebido your business."   [34:10]  "Um, profit is better than revenue. My business now does 50% less uh revenue than we did at its peak and infinitely more profit uh than we did when we were losing money."   [34:28]  "And so make uh a lot of people take pride in a million dollars a month. I I had a million dollars a month. I got one of these trophies and meanwhile my business was losing money. So you got to make sure that your business that a million dollars a month means that you're making 25 50% net income margin on that as well."   [34:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Scaling Fixed Headcount = Death Wish"   NÚMEROS:  125 people no pico, 3 pessoas no DEI department, $1.2M/month payroll, meta <10 pessoas para $1M/month   VISUAL:  Perigos de scaling com headcount fixo   [35:00]  "Um scaling fixed headcount is a death wish. Massive payroll is a massive liability. Um at my peak I had 125 people. We had three people for the de andi department." "We had a fuck de andi department in my in my agency. People sitting there doing nothing."   [35:25]  "Um, and when you have a lot of people, you have a lot of management layers. I got further away from the people that did the work. I got further away from our customers. I It was harder to make the right decisions. You have these middle managers now that are hiring the people and all of a sudden you have BNC talent coming into your company."   [35:50]  "Um, and so if I could do it all over again, I'd figure out how to get to a million dollars a month with less than 10 people. That's what I'm doing with my SAS business."   [36:08]  "Um, just how do we be more efficient? How do we use automation? AI is out there now that can do a lot of fuck fuck that a low-level entry- level employee what you'd pay to do."   [36:25]  "Um, how do we how are we able to do that so we can make more money and be more efficient and also not have this big raincloud hanging overview. At one point I had like $1.2 million a month payroll."   [36:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Debt - Pros and Cons"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lições sobre debt financing   [36:50]  "Um, and then uh and then debt pros and cons. Uh, the refinance debt that I took uh amazing. If anyone's going to go and get debt, get a traditional bank loan. They have better interest rates. collateralize with your assets. You need a schedule of assets to collateralize. And uh that's the only way that I would do debt. Now"   [37:15]  "um my flight got delayed for 30 minutes, so I can spend some time taking questions and discussion. Jeremy, if you're cool with that. Uh thanks y'all."     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:   Current State (2024/2025): $12.4M annual agency revenue (~$1M/month) $2.6M net income (20-25% margin) $640K average annual client retainer 5 clients paying >$1M/year 45 full-time employees (US/Canada, fully remote) $150K/month personal cash flow (quarterly distributions) $461K Q4 2024 distribution received $500K/quarter projected going forward Only 1 board meeting per quarter involvement   Historical Journey: 2019:  $600K revenue, started with $3K in bank, $100/hour → $5K/month retainers, hit $20K/month, then $1M/year in 8 months 2020:  $2.2M revenue, added partner at 10% for $250K ($2.6M valuation) 2021:  $6.8M revenue 2022 (Peak):  $21M revenue, $100K profit at $1.9M/month revenue,  -$1.6M net loss , 125 employees 2023:  Recovery phase 2024:  $12.4M revenue, 45 employees, restructured   The Crisis (2022): Lost $1.6M in one year -$200K/month burn rate 75 layoffs over 2 waves ~$1M paid in severances $4M debt raised (April 2022, 15% interest, mezzanine) Company technically insolvent Clients breaking annual agreements Marketing budgets cut first in SaaS winter   Debt Journey: Original:  $4M at 15% interest, interest-only first year, $180K/month payments later Refinanced (Summer 2024):  Traditional bank, $24K/month payments Impact:  Unlocked $150K/month net income to shareholders Covenants:  Max $400K combined salary for partners, no distributions allowed until refinance   SaaS Company (PTO - launched Jan 2024): $1.2M first year revenue $2.5-3M projected year 2 $50K/month profit currently $80-85K/month revenue currently Goal: $5-10M revenue → $50M+ exit <10 people target team   Other Investments: $25K/month passive from small business/agency investments Public market investments Portfolio diversification strategy   Hiring Evolution: First hire at $50K/month: video editor Second hire: advertising person Peak: 125 employees, $1.2M/month payroll Had 3-person DEI department Current: 45 employees Goal: <10 people to $1M/month with AI/automation   Pricing Evolution: Started: $100/hour Early: $2K-5K/month retainers Growth: $40K-50K/month retainers Current: $640K average annual retainer Focus: Million-dollar annual clients only   Personal Habits Foundation: Sold car, couldn't afford insurance/parking Started taking subway Consistent sleep schedule Morning workouts daily 7 days/week work schedule No weekend partying Implemented before making money   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:   Content Strategy: Started LinkedIn posting April 2019 First podcast episode: First week of COVID lockdown (March 2020) Peak: 6 podcast episodes/week Current: 3 podcast episodes/week Short-form content 7 days/week on LinkedIn Expanded to TikTok and YouTube 3 hours/week recording 30 minutes/day posting/producing Highest ROI activity weekly "Find channels where rich people hang out" Fastest growing B2B digital agency ever (per LinkedIn)   First YouTube Video Lessons: March 27, 2020 Used AirPods (bad production) Don't let production quality slow you down Quality of information > production value Get better incredibly fast by doing daily   ICP Evolution: Started: All over the place (ecom, professional services, agencies, software) Evolved to: High-growth venture/PE-funded software companies Reasoning: They get tons of investor funding that flows to agencies Risk: When investor faucet turns off, agency faucet turns off too Current: $50M-500M-$1B revenue software companies Some publicly traded, most PE-owned Million-dollar annual clients only focus   Service Expansion Strategy: Started: Media buying only Problem: Clients sucked at creative/content Added: Creative and content services Added: Analytics Result: Increased retainers from $10K → $40-50K/month Philosophy: Find ways to increase retainers   Market Movement: Started: Seed and Series A companies Problem: No money, bad products, don't know what to do, churn after 6 months Solution: Move upmarket to mature companies Sweet spot: $50M-500M revenue companies   First Client Acquisition: First 3 clients: Personal network, people who trusted him Then: LinkedIn organic content Later: Podcast drove explosive growth   Partner Structure: Added partner early 2020 SOLD 10% equity (didn't give away)  - key lesson $250K cash for 10% at $2.6M valuation January 2024: Granted additional 16.5% (total 26.5%) Incentivized with 8-figure exit on 3-year plan Partner now CEO, Chris is owner only Used equity to buy back all his time   Debt Strategy Lessons: Bad Debt:  Mezzanine at 15% interest Good Debt:  Traditional bank loan, collateralized with assets Need schedule of assets to collateralize Better interest rates with traditional banks Original saved company from bankruptcy Refinance unlocked massive cash flow   The Jeremy Call (August 2023) - Turning Point: "Bro, you got a $15 million business. Why burn it down?" "You're doing a million dollars a month" "Pick a fucking lane: cash flow it OR build to sell. You're in the middle doing neither." Changed entire agency strategy after this call "Paid off a trillion times" for mastermind membership   2022 SaaS Winter Context: Salesforce stock bottomed December 2022 All customers instructed to stop spending Marketing/agencies cut first (before layoffs) Clients breaking annual agreements "We can't pay you next month, fuck off" Massive churn wave   Crisis Management: Two waves of layoffs Almost $1M in severances paid Losing $200K/month Would lay off people → more clients churn → repeat Downward spiral $4M debt was lifeline that prevented bankruptcy   December 20, 2022 (Birthday) - Lowest Point: First time contemplated giving up Company technically insolvent $15M revenue but underwater balance sheet Had taken no money out for 3 years Making basically no money personally Conflict with business partner Tried trading, spent $100K+, made nothing Two attempted lawsuits from vendors Giving up = bankruptcy (owed bank money without assets to pay)   Recovery Strategy: Joined Inner Circle April 2023 August 2023 Jeremy call = life change Picked lane: Cash flow the agency Put partner in charge as CEO (Jan 2024) Started SaaS company (Jan 2024) Refinanced debt (Summer 2024) Focused on million-dollar clients only   ESTRUTURA:   Key Learnings:   Owner ≠ Operator Don't have to be CEO if you own majority Way better when someone else is CEO Pride was holding him back Didn't know this was possible   First Business = Stepping Stone Doesn't have to be only business Use for: industry reputation, experience, financial security Then use it to try for big winner   Organic Content = Massive Asset Works for life Tons know who you are Trust built 3 hours/week recording 30 minutes/day posting Highest ROI activity Drives all businesses   Cash Flow vs Enterprise Value Agencies valued on EBITDA Software companies valued on revenue growth (can lose money) Got caught thinking: grow revenue even with losses = valuable NOT TRUE FOR AGENCIES Pick a lane that matches your business type   Profit > Revenue Now does 50% less revenue than peak Infinitely more profit $1M/month with losses = worthless trophy Need 25-50% net income margin   Scaling Fixed Headcount = Death Wish 125 people = massive problems Had 3-person DEI department doing nothing Management layers separate you from work/customers Middle managers hire B/C talent $1.2M/month payroll = huge liability Goal: $1M/month with <10 people using AI/automation   Take Profit Out Continuously Biggest mistake:  Reinvested everything for 3 years Should: Get profit → take out → stash in other investments → repeat Don't tie all net worth in one illiquid asset Derisk continuously Build balanced portfolio $250K from 10% sale started this   Don't Give Equity Away Once you create value,  MAKE THEM BUY IT Don't give it away Partner bought 10% for $250K   You're Not An Entrepreneur Until Punched In Mouth 3 years sunshine and rainbows Then 2-3 years getting punched Be ready for it It's coming   Work On Yourself First Foundation before money/success Consistent sleep Morning workouts No weekend partying 7 days/week work Business follows self-improvement   Financial Model Differences: Agencies:  Valued on EBITDA, need profit SaaS:  Valued on revenue/growth, can lose money and increase value Services:  50% less revenue but infinitely more profit possible Software:  $5-10M revenue → $50M+ exit possible   The Two Lawsuits: Two software vendors (non-customer partners) Tried to sue during crisis period Part of the "a lot of other fuck happened"   Portfolio Diversification: Agency: $150K/month (quarterly) SaaS: $50K/month profit Passive investments: $25K/month Public markets: Additional Total: ~$225K/month+ across portfolio   Efficiency Philosophy: More people = more problems Only hire when absolutely needed Get as efficient as possible Get as profitable as possible Use AI/automation for low-level work <10 people to $1M/month goal   DADOS FINANCEIROS:   Revenue Journey: 2019: $600K ($50K/month average) 2020: $2.2M 2021: $6.8M 2022: $21M (peak) 2023: ~$15M (recovery) 2024: $12.4M (profitable)   Profit Journey: Early years: Profitable and scaling 2022: -$1.6M loss 2023: Almost recovered to breakeven 2024: $2.6M net income (20-25% margin) 2025: Projected $3M+ (25%+ margin)   Personal Compensation Evolution: Started: $3K in bank Early: $20K/month = $240K/year (felt amazing) Goal: $1M/year (hit in 8 months) Crisis: Basically $0 for years Covenant: Max $400K salary Current: $150K/month ($1.8M/year) passive   Debt Numbers: Original: $4M at 15% interest Interest-only first year Payments increased every 12 months Balloon payment: $1.6-2M at end Peak payments: $180K/month Refinanced payments: $24K/month Unlocked: $156K/month cash flow difference   Employee Count: Started: Solo At $50K/month: First 2 hires Peak: 125 employees Layoffs: 75 people Current: 45 employees Goal: <10 employees   Client Economics: Early: $2K/month contracts Growth: $5K/month retainers Scale: $40-50K/month retainers Current average: $640K/year ($53K/month) 5 clients: >$1M/year each Focus: Million-dollar annual clients only   SaaS Company (PTO): Year 1: $1.2M revenue Year 2 projection: $2.5-3M Current: $80-85K/month revenue Current: $50K/month profit Goal: $5-10M revenue Exit target: $50M+   Investment Returns: Other businesses: $25K/month passive Public markets: Undisclosed Total passive: $175K/month minimum   Cash Flow Analysis: Peak revenue: $1.9M/month Peak profit: $100K/month (5.3% margin) - TERRIBLE Current revenue: ~$1M/month Current profit: ~$220K/month (22% margin) - GREAT 50% less revenue, infinitely more profit   Severance Costs: Total: ~$1M 75 people laid off Two waves Without $4M debt: Would have given $0 severances   Balance Sheet: Peak crisis: Negative assets/liabilities ratio (insolvent) $5M in bank with debt → $3M after interest/payments End 2023: Almost recovered 2024: Positive and growing   Payroll Analysis: Peak: $1.2M/month 125 employees Multiple management layers 3-person DEI department Current: Significantly lower with 45 people   ROI Calculations: Content: 3 hours/week + 30 min/day = ~6 hours/week Return: Drives all business acquisition Highest ROI activity in entire week Lifetime asset   Valuation Comparisons: Agency at $2.6M valuation (2020): Sold 10% for $250K Agency current: Building for cash flow, not exit SaaS target: $5-10M revenue → $50M+ exit (5-10x revenue multiple) Services: ~1-3x EBITDA multiple Software: 5-10x+ revenue multiple   DURAÇÃO:  37:15  FORMATO:  Apresentação presencial em mastermind
